From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Standard defining codes for currencies

"Currency code" redirects here; not to be confused with [Currency symbol](/wiki/Currency_symbol "Currency symbol").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Flugschein.JPG/250px-Flugschein.JPG)](/wiki/File:Flugschein.JPG)

An airline ticket showing the price with ISO 4217 code "EUR" (*bottom left*) and not with euro [currency sign](/wiki/Currency_symbol "Currency symbol") "€"

**ISO 4217** is a standard published by the [International Organization for Standardization](/wiki/International_Organization_for_Standardization "International Organization for Standardization") (ISO) that defines alpha codes and numeric codes for the representation of currencies and provides information about the relationships between individual currencies and their minor units. This data is published in three tables:[[1]](#cite_note-SOS217-1)

* Table A.1 – *Current currency & funds code list*[[1]](#cite_note-SOS217-1)
* Table A.2 – *Current funds codes*[[1]](#cite_note-SOS217-1)
* Table A.3 – *List of codes for historic denominations of currencies & funds*[[1]](#cite_note-SOS217-1)

The first edition of ISO 4217 was published in 1978. The tables, history and ongoing discussion are maintained by [SIX Group](/wiki/SIX_Group "SIX Group") on behalf of [ISO](/wiki/International_Organization_for_Standardization "International Organization for Standardization") and the [Swiss Association for Standardization](/wiki/Swiss_Association_for_Standardization "Swiss Association for Standardization").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

The ISO 4217 code list is used in [banking](/wiki/Bank "Bank") and business globally. In many countries, the ISO 4217 alpha codes for the more common currencies are so well known publicly that exchange rates published in newspapers or posted in banks use only these to delineate the currencies, instead of translated currency names or ambiguous [currency symbols](/wiki/Currency_symbol "Currency symbol"). ISO 4217 alpha codes are used on airline tickets and international train tickets to remove any ambiguity about the price.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ISO_4217&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

In 1973, the ISO Technical Committee 68 decided to develop codes for the representation of currencies and funds for use in any application of trade, commerce or banking. At the 17th session (February 1978), the related [UN](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations")/[ECE](/wiki/United_Nations_Economic_Commission_for_Europe "United Nations Economic Commission for Europe") Group of Experts agreed that the three-letter alphabetic codes for International Standard ISO 4217, "Codes for the representation of currencies and funds", would be suitable for use in international trade.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Over time, new currencies are created and old currencies are discontinued. Such changes usually originate from the formation of new countries, treaties between countries on shared currencies or monetary unions, or [redenomination](/wiki/Redenomination "Redenomination") from an existing currency due to excessive inflation. As a result, the list of codes must be updated from time to time. The ISO 4217 maintenance agency is responsible for maintaining the list of codes.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Types of codes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ISO_4217&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Types of codes")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/South_East_Asia_Exchange_Rates_%286031878489%29.jpg/250px-South_East_Asia_Exchange_Rates_%286031878489%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:South_East_Asia_Exchange_Rates_(6031878489).jpg)

A list of exchange rates for various base currencies given by a [money changer](/wiki/Money_changer "Money changer") in Thailand, with the Thailand Baht as the counter (or quote) currency. Note that the code for the [South Korean won](/wiki/South_Korean_won "South Korean won") is displayed incorrectly; it should be KRW.

### National currencies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ISO_4217&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: National currencies")]

In the case of national currencies, the first two letters of the alpha code are the two letters of the [ISO 3166-1 alpha-2](/wiki/ISO_3166-1_alpha-2 "ISO 3166-1 alpha-2") [country code](/wiki/Country_code "Country code") and the third is usually the initial of the currency's main unit.[[4]](#cite_note-4) So [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan")'s currency code is [JPY](/wiki/Japanese_yen "Japanese yen"): "JP" for Japan and "Y" for [yen](/wiki/Japanese_yen "Japanese yen"). This eliminates the problem caused by the names *[dollar](/wiki/Dollar "Dollar")*, *[franc](/wiki/Franc "Franc")*, *[peso](/wiki/Peso "Peso")*, and *[pound](/wiki/Pound_(currency) "Pound (currency)")* being used in many countries, each having significantly differing values.

In some cases, the third letter of the alpha code is not the initial letter of a currency unit name. There may be a number of reasons for this:

* It is considered important that the code of a completely new currency be highly mnemonic if possible. An example is the assignment of the code EUR to the euro. ISO 4217 amendment 94,[[5]](#cite_note-5) which created this code, states "The code element 'EU' has been reserved by the ISO 3166 Maintenance Agency for use within ISO 4217 where 'R' has been appended to make an acceptable mnemonic code." Here the R comes from the third letter in the word "euro".
* The currency in question is replacing another currency of the same name, due to revaluation. So that the two currencies have different codes, a different third letter must be chosen for the code of the new currency. In some cases, the third letter is the initial for "new" in that country's language, to distinguish it from an older currency that was revalued; the code sometimes outlasts the usage of the term "new" itself (for example, the code for the [Mexican peso](/wiki/Mexican_peso "Mexican peso") is MXN, reflecting its 1993 revaluation). Another solution to a revalued currency having the same name as its predecessor is to choose a third letter which results in a 3-letter code with mnemonic significance. For example, the [Russian ruble](/wiki/Russian_ruble "Russian ruble") changed from RUR to RUB following a revaluation, where the B comes from the third letter in the word "ruble".[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### X currencies (funds, precious metals, supranationals, other)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ISO_4217&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: X currencies (funds, precious metals, supranationals, other)")]

In addition to codes for most active national currencies ISO 4217 provides codes for "supranational" currencies, procedural purposes, and several things which are "similar to" currencies:

* Codes for the [precious metals](/wiki/Precious_metal "Precious metal") [gold](/wiki/Gold_as_an_investment "Gold as an investment") (XAU), [silver](/wiki/Silver_as_an_investment "Silver as an investment") (XAG), [palladium](/wiki/Palladium_as_an_investment "Palladium as an investment") (XPD), and [platinum](/wiki/Platinum_as_an_investment "Platinum as an investment") (XPT) are formed by prefixing the element's chemical symbol with the letter "X". These "currency units" are defined as one [troy ounce](/wiki/Troy_ounce "Troy ounce") of the specified metal.
* The code XTS is reserved for use in testing.
* The code XXX is used to denote a "transaction" involving no currency.
* There are also codes specifying certain monetary instruments used in international finance, e.g. XDR is the symbol for [special drawing right](/wiki/Special_drawing_right "Special drawing right") issued by the [International Monetary Fund](/wiki/International_Monetary_Fund "International Monetary Fund").
* The codes for most [supranational](/wiki/Supranational_currency "Supranational currency") currencies, such as the [East Caribbean dollar](/wiki/East_Caribbean_dollar "East Caribbean dollar"), the [CFP franc](/wiki/CFP_franc "CFP franc"), the [CFA franc](/wiki/CFAF "CFAF") BEAC, and the CFA franc BCEAO. The predecessor to the euro, the [European Currency Unit](/wiki/European_Currency_Unit "European Currency Unit") (ECU), had the code XEU.

The use of the initial letter "X" for these purposes is facilitated by the [ISO 3166 rule](/wiki/ISO_3166#Codes_beginning_with_"X" "ISO 3166") that no official country code beginning with X will ever be assigned.

The inclusion of the EU (denoting the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union")) in the [ISO 3166-1](/wiki/ISO_3166-1 "ISO 3166-1") reserved codes list allows the [euro](/wiki/Euro "Euro") to be coded as EUR rather than assigned a code beginning with X, even though it is a supranational currency.

### Numeric codes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ISO_4217&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Numeric codes")]

ISO 4217 also assigns a three-digit numeric code to each currency. This numeric code is usually the same as the numeric code assigned to the corresponding country by [ISO 3166-1](/wiki/ISO_3166-1 "ISO 3166-1"). For example, USD ([United States dollar](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar")) has numeric code 840 which is also the ISO 3166-1 code for "US" (United States).

According to UN/CEFACT recommendation 9, paragraphs 8–9 ECE/TRADE/203, 1996:[[22]](#cite_note-rec09_ecetrd203-29)

:   8. In applications where monetary resources associated with a currency (i.e. funds) need not be specified and where a field identifier indicating currency is used, the first two (leftmost) characters are sufficient to identify a currency—example: US for United States dollars for general, unspecified purposes where a field identifier indicating currency is present. (A field identifier can be a preprinted field heading in an aligned document or a similarly-agreed application in electronic transmission of data.)
:   9. In applications where there is a need to distinguish between types of currencies, or where funds are required as in the banking environment, or where there is no field identifier, the third (rightmost) character of the alphabetic code is an indicator, preferably mnemonic, derived from the name of the major currency unit or fund—example: USD for general, unspecified purposes; USN for United States dollar next-day funds, and USS for funds which are immediately available for Federal Reserve transfer, withdrawal in cash or transfer in like funds (same-day funds). Since there is no need for such a distinction in international trade applications, the funds codes have not been included in the Annex to the present Recommendation.

## Currency details

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ISO_4217&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Currency details")]

### Minor unit fractions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ISO_4217&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Minor unit fractions")]

The 2008 (7th) edition of ISO 4217 says the following about minor units of currency:

> Requirements sometimes arise for values to be expressed in terms of minor units of currency. When this occurs, it is necessary to know the decimal relationship that exists between the currency concerned and its minor unit. This information has therefore been included in this International Standard and is shown in the column headed "Minor unit" in Tables A.1 and A.2; "0" means that there is no minor unit for that currency, whereas "1", "2" and "3" signify a ratio of 10:1, 100:1 and 1000:1 respectively. The names of the minor units are not given.

Examples for the ratios of 100:1 and 1000:1 include the United States dollar and the [Bahraini dinar](/wiki/Bahraini_dinar "Bahraini dinar"), for which the column headed "Minor unit" shows "2" and "3", respectively. As of 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=ISO_4217&action=edit), two currencies have non-decimal ratios, the [Mauritanian ouguiya](/wiki/Mauritanian_ouguiya "Mauritanian ouguiya") and the [Malagasy ariary](/wiki/Malagasy_ariary "Malagasy ariary"); in both cases the ratio is 5:1. For these, the "Minor unit" column shows the number "2". Some currencies, such as the [Burundian franc](/wiki/Burundian_franc "Burundian franc"), do not in practice have any minor currency unit at all. These show the number "0", as with currencies whose minor units are unused due to negligible value.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Code position in amount formatting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ISO_4217&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Code position in amount formatting")]

The ISO 4217 standard does not regulate either the spacing, prefixing or suffixing in usage of currency codes. The [style guide](/wiki/Style_guide "Style guide") of the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union")'s Publication Office declares that, for texts issued by or through the Commission in [English](/wiki/English_language_in_England "English language in England"), [Irish](/wiki/Irish_language "Irish language"), [Latvian](/wiki/Latvian_language "Latvian language"), and [Maltese](/wiki/Maltese_language "Maltese language"), the ISO 4217 code is to be followed by a "hard space" ([non-breaking space](/wiki/Non-breaking_space "Non-breaking space")) and the amount:[[47]](#cite_note-57)

:   *a sum of EUR 30*

and for texts in [Bulgarian](/wiki/Bulgarian_language "Bulgarian language"), [Croatian](/wiki/Croatian_language "Croatian language"), [Czech](/wiki/Czech_language "Czech language"), [Danish](/wiki/Danish_language "Danish language"), [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"), [Estonian](/wiki/Estonian_language "Estonian language"), [Finnish](/wiki/Finnish_language "Finnish language"), [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"), [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"), [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language"), [Hungarian](/wiki/Hungarian_language "Hungarian language"), [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"), [Lithuanian](/wiki/Lithuanian_language "Lithuanian language"), [Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language"), [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"), [Romanian](/wiki/Romanian_language "Romanian language"), [Slovak](/wiki/Slovak_language "Slovak language"), [Slovene](/wiki/Slovene_language "Slovene language"), [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"), and [Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language") the order is reversed; the amount is followed by a non-breaking space and the ISO 4217 code:

:   *une somme de 30 EUR*

As illustrated, the order is determined not by the currency but by the native language of the document context.

### USD, USN: two US currency codes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ISO_4217&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: USD, USN: two US currency codes")]

The [US dollar](/wiki/US_dollar "US dollar") has two codes assigned: USD and USN ("US dollar next day"[*[definition needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]). The USS (same day) code is not in use any longer, and was removed from the list of active ISO 4217 codes in March 2014.

## Non ISO 4217 currencies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ISO_4217&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Non ISO 4217 currencies")]

### Currencies without ISO 4217 currency codes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ISO_4217&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Currencies without ISO 4217 currency codes")]

A number of active currencies do not have an ISO 4217 code, because they may be:

1. a minor currency pegged at par (1:1) to a larger currency, even if independently regulated,
2. a currency only used for commemorative banknotes or coins, or
3. a currency of an [unrecognized or partially recognized state](/wiki/List_of_states_with_limited_recognition "List of states with limited recognition").

These currencies include:

* [Abkhazian apsar](/wiki/Abkhazian_apsar "Abkhazian apsar") (state of issue is viewed as *[de jure](/wiki/De_jure "De jure")* part of [Georgia](/wiki/Georgia_(country) "Georgia (country)"))
* [Alderney pound](/wiki/Alderney_pound "Alderney pound") (1:1 [pegged](/wiki/Fixed_exchange_rate "Fixed exchange rate") to sterling)
* [Cook Islands dollar](/wiki/Cook_Islands_dollar "Cook Islands dollar") (1:1 pegged to the New Zealand dollar)
* [Faroese króna](/wiki/Faroese_kr%C3%B3na "Faroese króna") (1:1 pegged to the Danish krone)
* [Guernsey pound](/wiki/Guernsey_pound "Guernsey pound") (1:1 pegged to sterling)
* [Isle of Man pound](/wiki/Isle_of_Man_pound "Isle of Man pound") (1:1 pegged to sterling)
* [Jersey pound](/wiki/Jersey_pound "Jersey pound") (1:1 pegged to sterling)
* [Kiribati dollar](/wiki/Kiribati_dollar "Kiribati dollar") (1:1 pegged to the Australian dollar)
* [Maltese scudo](/wiki/Maltese_scudo "Maltese scudo") (1:0.24 pegged to the [euro](/wiki/Euro "Euro")[[48]](#cite_note-58)) (Currency of the [Sovereign Order of Malta](/wiki/Sovereign_Order_of_Malta "Sovereign Order of Malta"); the [Republic of Malta](/wiki/Republic_of_Malta "Republic of Malta") uses the euro.)
* [Sahrawi peseta](/wiki/Sahrawi_peseta "Sahrawi peseta") (pegged to the euro), sometimes given the code "EHP" but this has not been assigned by the ISO
* [Somaliland shilling](/wiki/Somaliland_shilling "Somaliland shilling") (state of issue is viewed as *de jure* part of [Somalia](/wiki/Somalia "Somalia"), exchange rate not fixed)
* [Transnistrian ruble](/wiki/Transnistrian_ruble "Transnistrian ruble") (state of issue is viewed as *de jure* part of [Moldova](/wiki/Moldova "Moldova"))
* [Tuvaluan dollar](/wiki/Tuvaluan_dollar "Tuvaluan dollar") (1:1 pegged to the Australian dollar)

See [Category:Fixed exchange rate](/wiki/Category:Fixed_exchange_rate "Category:Fixed exchange rate") for a list of all currently pegged currencies.

### Non-standard codes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ISO_4217&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Non-standard codes")]

Despite having no presence or status in the standard, [three letter acronyms](/wiki/Three_letter_acronym "Three letter acronym") that resemble ISO 4217 coding are sometimes used locally or commercially to represent *de facto* currencies or currency instruments.

Active abbreviations resembling ISO 4217 currency codes

### Unofficial codes for minor units of currency

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ISO_4217&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Unofficial codes for minor units of currency")]

Minor units of currency (also known as currency subdivisions or currency subunits) are often used for pricing and trading company shares and other assets, such as energy,[[73]](#cite_note-Agency_for_the_Cooperation_of_Energy_Regulators_(ACER)-86) but are not assigned codes by ISO 4217. Two conventions for representing minor units are in widespread use:

* Replacing the third letter of the ISO 4217 Code of the parent currency with an upper-case "X". Examples are GBX[[73]](#cite_note-Agency_for_the_Cooperation_of_Energy_Regulators_(ACER)-86)[[74]](#cite_note-London_Stock_Exchange-87) for the Penny Sterling, USX for the US Cent, and EUX[[74]](#cite_note-London_Stock_Exchange-87)[[73]](#cite_note-Agency_for_the_Cooperation_of_Energy_Regulators_(ACER)-86) for the Euro Cent.
* Replacing the third letter of the ISO 4217 Code of the parent currency with the first letter of the name of a minor unit, using lower-case. Examples are GBp[[75]](#cite_note-OnixS-88) for Penny Sterling, USc[[75]](#cite_note-OnixS-88) for the US Cent, and EUc[[75]](#cite_note-OnixS-88) for the Euro Cent.

A third convention is similar to the second one but uses an upper-case letter, e.g. ZAC[[76]](#cite_note-89) for the South African Cent.

### Cryptocurrencies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ISO_4217&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Cryptocurrencies")]

See also: [List of cryptocurrencies](/wiki/List_of_cryptocurrencies "List of cryptocurrencies")

[Cryptocurrencies](/wiki/Cryptocurrency "Cryptocurrency") have *not* been assigned an ISO 4217 code.[[77]](#cite_note-90) However, some cryptocurrencies and [cryptocurrency exchanges](/wiki/Cryptocurrency_exchange "Cryptocurrency exchange") use a three-letter acronym that resemble an ISO 4217 code.
